label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure this out. So Jane was born on the last day of February in 2001. Wait, February's last day is usually the 28th, unless it's a leap year, which would make it the 29th. But 2001 isn't a leap year because leap years are divisible by 4. 2000 was a leap year, then 2004 would be the next. So 2001's February had 28 days. So Jane was born on 02/28/2001. Today is her 16th birthday. So that means today's date must be her birthdate in 2017, since 2001 + 16 = 2017. So today is 02/28/2017. Now the question is asking, what was the date 10 days ago from today? So subtract 10 days from 02/28/2017. Let me count backwards. Starting from the 28th, 1 day before is the 27th, then 26th, 25th... So subtracting 10 days. Let's see: 28 minus 10 is 18. So that would be 02/18/2017. But wait, is there anything about February 2017 that I need to consider? Let me check how many days February had in 2017. 2017 isn't a leap year (since 2016 was, then 2020), so February had 28 days. So yes, February 18 is a valid date. Looking at the options, option B is 02/18/2017. But let me double-check. If today is 02/28/2017, then 10 days prior would be the 18th. Let me count again: 28 (today) minus 10 days. If I go back 1 day: 27, 2, 26, 3, 25, 4, 24, 5, 23, 6, 22, 7, 21, 8, 20, 9, 19, 10... Wait, no, that's if I count each day. Wait, no, if today is the 28th, then 1 day ago was the 27th. So 10 days ago would be 28 - 10 = 18th. So yes, 02/18/2017. That's option B. But wait, hold on. Let me check another way. Let's say from the 18th to the 28th is 10 days. So 28 - 18 = 10. So yes, that's correct. So 10 days ago would be February 18th. Therefore, the answer should be B. Let me check the options again to make sure I didn't mix up any numbers. The options are: (A) 02/28/2017 (that's today, so no) (B) 02/18/2017 (10 days ago) (C) 03/18/2017 (March? That would be way later, not earlier) (D) 02/13/2017 (only 15 days before) (E) 02/25/2017 (only 3 days before) (F) 03/04/2017 (March again, which is after February, so not correct) So yes, option B is the correct answer. But let me just make sure there's no trick here. For example, if Jane was born on the last day of February, but if someone's birthday is on February 29th (leap year), then on non-leap years, their birthday might be considered either February 28th or March 1st.
